severityScales.name: IRLS (International RLS Study Group Rating Scale)
severityScales.description: 10-item self-administered scale; scores 0-40; 0-10 mild, 11-20 moderate, 21-30 severe, 31-40 very severe

severityScales.name: Clinical Global Impression (CGI-S)
severityScales.description: Clinician-rated severity from 1 (normal) to 7 (very severely ill)

severityScales.name: RLS-6 Scale
severityScales.description: 6-item scale assessing severity of RLS at different times of day and during rest/activity

frequencyCriteria: Intermittent RLS: symptoms occur less than twice weekly, causing mild to moderate distress
Chronic persistent RLS: symptoms occur at least twice weekly, causing moderate to severe distress

essentialDiagnosticCriteria: Urge to move the legs, usually accompanied or caused by uncomfortable and unpleasant sensations in the legs
Symptoms begin or worsen during periods of rest or inactivity (e.g., sitting, lying down)
Symptoms are partially or totally relieved by movement (e.g., walking, stretching, rubbing legs)
Symptoms are worse in the evening or night than during the day, or occur only in the evening or nighttime
symptomCharacteristics: Unpleasant sensations described as: crawling, creeping, pulling, gnawing, aching, throbbing, itching, or electric sensations
Sensations deep within the legs (not in the skin), often between knee and ankle
May involve thighs, feet, and in severe cases, arms or other body parts
Bilateral limb involvement is common; arms affected in up to 50% of severe cases
circadianPattern: Symptoms peak in the evening and during the night (typically between 10 PM and 2 AM)
Symptom nadir in the early morning hours
This circadian pattern is a defining feature and reflects underlying dopaminergic circadian variation
associatedFeatures: Sleep disturbance: difficulty initiating and maintaining sleep due to urge to move
Daytime fatigue, somnolence, and cognitive impairment secondary to sleep disruption
Involuntary limb movements while awake or during sleep (periodic limb movements)
Restlessness: inability to sit still during meetings, car rides, flights, watching movies
Compulsive need to walk, pace, stretch, or massage legs for relief
Anxiety or distress from inability to rest
naturalHistory: May begin at any age; earlier onset (<45 years) associates with more severe, familial, and slowly progressive course
Later onset (>45 years) may be more rapidly progressive and associated with secondary causes
Periodic remissions and exacerbations are common
Pregnancy-related RLS typically resolves after delivery

examFeatures.generalPhysicalExam: Normal neurologic examination in idiopathic RLS; examination is crucial to exclude mimics and secondary causes
neurologicExam: Motor examination: normal strength, tone, and coordination
Sensory examination: normal sensation (no objective sensory deficits or level)
Reflexes: normal deep tendon reflexes (may be reduced in peripheral neuropathy-associated RLS)
Signs of peripheral neuropathy: reduced distal sensation, reduced ankle reflexes, distal weakness (if secondary to neuropathy)
Signs of iron deficiency: pallor, cheilitis, koilonychia
Signs of Parkinson disease: bradykinesia, rigidity, tremor, postural instability (RLS is not responsive to these and does not progress to PD)
observationalFeatures: Visible restlessness during examination: shifting position, crossing/uncrossing legs, walking around room
Involuntary or voluntary leg movements while seated on examination table
Periodic limb movements: repetitive, stereotyped flexion of hip, knee, and ankle (may be observed if patient falls asleep during examination)

riskFactors: Family history: 50-60% of idiopathic cases have positive family history; autosomal dominant inheritance pattern in some families
Iron deficiency: low serum ferritin (<75 ng/mL) even without anemia is strongly associated
Pregnancy: 20-30% prevalence in third trimester; usually resolves within weeks of delivery
End-stage renal disease: 20-70% in hemodialysis or peritoneal dialysis
Peripheral neuropathy: diabetic, hereditary, or acquired neuropathies associated with increased RLS prevalence
Diabetes mellitus: associated via small fiber neuropathy and metabolic disturbances
Multiple sclerosis: increased prevalence, possibly due to spinal cord lesions disrupting descending inhibition
Medications that exacerbate RLS: SSRIs, SNRIs, mirtazapine, antipsychotics (dopamine-blockers), antihistamines, lithium, caffeine
Caffeine, alcohol, and nicotine use can exacerbate symptoms
Female sex: 1.5-2 times more common than in males
Age: prevalence increases with age but symptoms may become less severe in elderly

subtypes.name: Primary (Idiopathic) RLS
subtypes.description: No identifiable underlying cause; strong genetic component
subtypes.details: Earlier age of onset (<45 years), more severe and progressive course, strong family history, generally lifelong condition; genetic variants in BTBD9, MEIS1, MAP2K5, PTPRD identified

subtypes.name: Secondary RLS
subtypes.description: Associated with an underlying medical condition or medication
subtypes.details: Later age of onset (>45 years), more rapid progression, less family history; resolves or improves with treatment of underlying cause

subtypes.name: Iron deficiency-related RLS
subtypes.description: RLS secondary to low iron stores (serum ferritin <75 ng/mL or transferrin saturation <20%)
subtypes.details: Most common secondary cause; iron therapy (oral or IV) may improve or resolve symptoms; iron is a cofactor for tyrosine hydroxylase in dopamine synthesis

subtypes.name: Pregnancy-associated RLS
subtypes.description: RLS onset or exacerbation during pregnancy, most commonly third trimester
subtypes.details: Prevalence 20-30%; likely multifactorial (iron deficiency, folate deficiency, hormonal changes, venous stasis); typically resolves within 4 weeks postpartum

subtypes.name: Uremic RLS
subtypes.description: RLS associated with chronic kidney disease and dialysis
subtypes.details: Prevalence 20-70% in dialysis patients; improves with renal transplantation; may respond to exercise, dopamine agonists, gabapentin, or IV iron

subtypes.name: Medication-induced RLS
subtypes.description: RLS exacerbated or induced by specific pharmacologic agents
subtypes.details: Common culprits: SSRIs, SNRIs, mitrazapine, antipsychotics, lithium, antihistamines, diphenhydramine; symptoms improve upon switching to bupropion (which does not worsen RLS) or discontinuing offending agent

mimics: Periodic limb movement disorder (PLMD): periodic limb movements during sleep without the subjective urge to move or sensory component
Peripheral neuropathy: constant burning, tingling, or numbness; not relieved by movement; not worse at rest or circadian; objective sensory loss on exam

Neurogenic claudication (spinal stenosis): back pain, leg pain on walking, relieved by sitting or bending forward; not worse at rest; vascular claudication: leg cramps on walking, relieved by standing still
Akathisia: subjective inner restlessness with objective fidgety movements; generalized (not just legs); drug-induced (antipsychotics); constant, not circadian; not relieved by walking
Positional discomfort: discomfort from lying in one position; relieved by repositioning without needing to walk
Leg cramps: sudden painful muscle contraction, palpable muscle hardness, not relieved by walking
Venous stasis: leg heaviness, swelling, varicose veins, not strictly circadian, not relieved by movement alone (elevation helps more)
Arthritis: joint pain, swelling, morning stiffness; not worse at rest or circadian
Growing pains in children: bilateral leg pain at night, but typically frontal shins and calves, not associated with strong urge to move
Hypnic jerks: brief myoclonic jerks at sleep onset, not associated with urge to move or sensory discomfort

bedside: Detailed history using IRLSSG Diagnostic Criteria (essential, as diagnosis is clinical)
IRLS Rating Scale administration for baseline severity
Sleep history: bedtime, sleep onset latency, wake after sleep onset, sleep quality
Medication review: particularly antidepressants, antihistamines, antipsychotics, caffeine, nicotine, alcohol
Family history: presence of RLS, PLMD, or sleep disorders in first-degree relatives
Peripheral vascular examination: pulses, varicose veins, edema
Neurologic examination: full motor, sensory, reflex, coordination, and gait assessment
laboratory: Serum ferritin: ESSENTIAL test in all RLS patients; goal >75 ng/mL for symptom improvement; <75 associated with increased symptom severity
Iron studies panel: serum iron, TIBC, transferrin saturation (<20% abnormal)
Complete blood count: rule out anemia
Serum creatinine and BUN: rule out renal failure (uremic RLS)
Fasting glucose or HbA1c: screen for diabetes mellitus
Vitamin B12, folate: deficiency may cause or exacerbate neuropathy and RLS symptoms
Magnesium, vitamin D: deficiency may exacerbate symptoms
Kidney function and electrolyte panel: baseline for medication safety
TSH: thyroid dysfunction may contribute to symptoms

imaging.indications: Not required for routine RLS diagnosis; indicated when: atypical presentation, suspected PLMD, suspected other sleep disorders (sleep apnea, narcolepsy), unresponsive to treatment, or research purposes
findings: Periodic limb movements during sleep (PLMS): >5/hour in children, >15/hour in adults support RLS diagnosis
Increased wake after sleep onset (maintenance insomnia)
Reduced total sleep time and sleep efficiency
PLMS index of >15/hour in adults is considered clinically significant

imaging.details: Patient lies in bed for 1 hour with legs extended; asked to remain still; severity of sensory discomfort and leg movements monitored
imaging.findings: Quantifies rest-induced symptoms; supports diagnosis when history uncertain
imaging.use: Diagnostic confirmation in research settings; may help differentiate from mimics

imaging.indications: Suspected peripheral neuropathy as secondary cause for RLS
imaging.findings: May show axonal or demyelinating peripheral neuropathy patterns; normal NCS/EMG does not exclude small fiber neuropathy
imaging.use: Rule out neuropathy as cause of RLS-mimicking symptoms

imaging.indications: Research context only; not clinically indicated for routine diagnosis
imaging.findings: Reduced iron content in substantia nigra and putamen on R2* mapping
imaging.use: Investigational; supports concept of regional brain iron deficiency in RLS

management.protocolReference: International Restless Legs Syndrome Study Group (IRLSSG) and European Academy of Neurology (EAN) guidelines (2022)
nonPharmacological: Discontinue or reduce exacerbating medications: SSRIs/SNRIs (switch to bupropion if needed), antihistamines, dopamine-blocking antipsychotics, mirtazapine
Iron supplementation: oral ferrous sulfate 325 mg with vitamin C, if serum ferritin <75 ng/mL; consider IV ferric carboxymaltose if oral iron inadequate or poorly tolerated
Moderate regular exercise: walking, swimming, or cycling 30-60 minutes daily; evening exercise particularly helpful
Avoid caffeine, alcohol, and nicotine, especially in the evening
Sleep hygiene: consistent bedtime, cool quiet dark room, avoid reading/watching TV in bed
Pre-bedtime activities: stretching, massage, warm bath, compression stockings, pneumatic compression devices
Mental distraction techniques: puzzles, games, conversation during rest periods

intermittentRLS.agents: Gabapentin 300-1200 mg, Gabapentin enacarbil 300-600 mg, or Pregabalin 75-300 mg
intermittentRLS.timing: Taken 1-2 hours before symptom onset, typically evening or bedtime
intermittentRLS.advantages: Low augmentation risk; improves sleep quality; treats comorbid pain
intermittentRLS.adverseEffects: Dizziness, somnolence, weight gain, peripheral edema, cognitive dulling

chronicPersistentRLS.class: Alpha-2-delta ligands (first-line for chronic persistent RLS)
chronicPersistentRLS.agents: Gabapentin 600-1200 mg, Pregabalin 150-300 mg, or Gabapentin enacarbil 600 mg
chronicPersistentRLS.note: Preferred first-line agents due to superior safety profile regarding augmentation
chronicPersistentRLS.dosing: Divided doses: afternoon dose for early evening symptoms, larger dose at bedtime

agents: Pramipexole: 0.125-0.75 mg daily, taken 2-3 hours before symptoms
Ropinirole: 0.25-4 mg daily, taken 1-3 hours before symptoms
Rotigotine transdermal patch: 1-3 mg/24 h, continuous delivery
risks: Augmentation: worsening of symptoms earlier in the day, spread to arms, increased severity -- occurs in 50-80% over time with high doses
Impulse control disorders: compulsive gambling, shopping, eating, hypersexuality
chronicPersistentRLS.restrictions: Use LOWEST effective dose; NEVER exceed maximum recommended doses; monitor for augmentation at each visit
chronicPersistentRLS.indications: Use when alpha-2-delta ligands fail or are contraindicated; use for short-term when goal-directed

refractoryRLS: Combination therapy: alpha-2-delta agent plus low-dose dopamine agonist (short-term only)
Opioids: prolonged-release oxycodone-naloxone: 5/2.5 mg to 20/10 mg daily; reserved for severe refractory RLS after failure of all other options
Benzodiazepines: clonazepam 0.5-2 mg for sleep; effective for sleep but not RLS sensory symptoms; tolerance risk
Iron therapy (IV): ferric carboxymaltose 1000 mg IV for patients with ferritin <75 ng/mL
Treat underlying cause if secondary RLS (renal transplant for uremic RLS, iron repletion)

intermittentRLS: As-needed low-dose dopamine agonist or alpha-2-delta ligand for symptom episodes
chronicPersistentRLS: Correct iron deficiency if present (ferritin >75 ng/mL target)
Alpha-2-delta ligand (gabapentin, gabapentin enacarbil, pregabalin) as first-line
If inadequate: switch to dopamine agonist at LOWEST effective dose
If augmentation develops: discontinue dopamine agonist, switch to alpha-2-delta ligand
If refractory: combination therapy, consider opioid under specialist supervision only

pathways: Step 1: Check ferritin - if <75 ng/mL, treat with iron supplementation (oral or IV)
Step 2: For intermittent RLS - non-pharmacologic measures plus as-needed medications
Step 3: For chronic persistent RLS - first-line alpha-2-delta ligand (gabapentin/pregabalin/gabapentin enacarbil)
Step 4: If inadequate response or contraindication to alpha-2-delta - low-dose dopamine agonist with strict augmentation monitoring
Step 5: If augmentation, impulse control disorder, or inadequate response - discontinue dopamine agonist, switch to alternative class
Step 6: For refractory severe RLS - specialist referral, consider combination therapy, low-dose opioid

primaryMechanisms: Brain iron deficiency: decreased iron in substantia nigra and other brain regions even with normal peripheral iron stores; iron is essential cofactor for tyrosine hydroxylase (rate-limiting step in dopamine synthesis)
Dopaminergic dysfunction: altered dopamine D2 receptor function; impaired presynaptic dopaminergic regulation; circadian dopamine rhythm disruption
Impaired descending inhibition: reduced A11 hypothalamospinal dopaminergic projections to spinal cord leads to spinal hyperexcitability
Spinal cord hyperexcitability: increased flexion reflex; reduced intracortical inhibition; enhanced sensorimotor integration
Genetic factors: variants in BTBD9, MEIS1, MAP2K5, PTPRD associated with increased susceptibility; MEIS1 involved in iron metabolism regulation
dopamineHypothesis: Not simply dopamine deficiency (unlike Parkinson disease) but rather dysregulation of dopamine signaling
Exogenous dopamine relieves symptoms acutely (consistent with clinical response to dopamine agonists)
Chronic dopamine treatment leads to augmentation (consistent with receptor sensitization or altered dopamine homeostasis)
Diurnal variation in dopamine function underlies circadian pattern of symptoms
ironDopamineConnection: Iron is required for dopamine synthesis (tyrosine hydroxylase cofactor) and function (D2 receptor signaling)

Brain iron deficiency leads to: reduced dopamine transporter expression, increased extracellular dopamine, altered D1 and D2 receptor balance, impaired intracellular iron trafficking (divalent metal transporter 1, transferrin receptor dysregulation)
geneticInsights: MEIS1: homeobox transcription factor regulating iron metabolism pathway
BTBD9: involved in circadian rhythm regulation and iron homeostasis
MAP2K5 and PTPRD: involved in neuronal development and synaptic function
These genes explain approximately 80% of RLS heritability in some populations

complications: Augmentation syndrome: dopamine agonist-induced worsening with earlier onset, greater intensity, broader anatomic distribution (arms, trunk); most common with high-dose dopamine agonists; can be severe and irreversible
Impulse control disorders: compulsive gambling, shopping, eating, hypersexuality associated with dopamine agonists
Severe insomnia and chronic sleep deprivation
Daytime fatigue, excessive daytime sleepiness, impaired daytime function
Depression and anxiety disorders: bidirectional relationship with RLS
Reduced quality of life: comparable to other chronic medical conditions (diabetes, arthritis)
Cardiovascular risk: possible association with hypertension and cardiovascular disease from sleep fragmentation and sympathetic hyperactivity
Cognitive impairment: attentional deficits and executive dysfunction from chronic sleep disruption
Impaired work performance and increased absenteeism
Relationship difficulties from nighttime restlessness and sleep disturbance
mustNotMiss: Always check serum ferritin in ALL RLS patients - iron deficiency is the most treatable secondary cause
Augmentation from dopamine agonists is common (40-80% over time) and requires immediate discontinuation of the offending agent
Do NOT start dopamine agonists at high doses or exceed recommended maximums (pramipexole >0.75 mg/day, ropinirole >4 mg/day) - this dramatically increases augmentation risk
SSRIs and SNRIs can trigger or worsen RLS - consider switching to bupropion which does not exacerbate RLS
Alpha-2-delta ligands (gabapentin, pregabalin) are now first-line for chronic persistent RLS due to lower augmentation risk
RLS in pregnancy requires non-pharmacologic treatment first; iron and folate supplementation; dopamine agonists and gabapentin caution
Opioids should only be used for severe refractory RLS after failure of all other treatments and under specialist supervision
emergencyPriority: LOW - Not life-threatening; causes significant distress and impairment of quality of life
diagnosticClues: Urge to move legs accompanied by unpleasant sensations, worse at rest, relieved by movement, circadian (evening/night worsening)
All four essential diagnostic criteria must be present for diagnosis
Symptoms improve with leg movement and return when movement ceases
Family history of RLS or periodic limb movements supports diagnosis
Circadian pattern: symptoms peak 10 PM to 2 AM, nadir early morning
Periodic limb movements during sleep or wakefulness on polysomnography
Low serum ferritin (<75 ng/mL) provides supportive but not required evidence
Dramatic response to dopamine agonists or gabapentinoids is supportive
Worsening with antihistamines or SSRIs is suggestive
exclusionClues: Symptoms not worse at rest or not relieved by movement suggests alternative diagnosis
No circadian variation (symptoms equal throughout day) argues against RLS
Isolated leg cramps or painful muscle contractions suggest nocturnal leg cramps or myopathy
Constant burning or tingling with objective sensory loss suggests peripheral neuropathy
Symptoms solely on walking with relief on bending forward suggests neurogenic claudication
Generalized inner restlessness without leg-specific urge suggests akathisia
Joint pain, swelling, or morning stiffness suggests arthritis
No improvement with dopamine agonists or alpha-2-delta ligands should prompt diagnostic reevaluation
Abnormal neurologic examination suggests secondary RLS or alternative diagnosis
